setting.note: Esther Greenwood spends a glittering, hollow month as a guest editor at a women's magazine in New York, staying at a women-only hotel and going through the motions of a life she cannot feel, before returning to her mother's house in the Boston suburbs and a breakdown. The New York half is closely mapped: the Amazon hotel is the real Barbizon, the magazine is Plath's own Mademoiselle, and the United Nations turns up on a date. The Massachusetts scenes of home and hospital run outside the setting.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Sylvia Plath, first published 1963. Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: The Bell Jar, Sylvia Plath (1963). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author's estate or publisher.

palette.note: The airless violet of the glass jar Esther feels sealed inside, stewing in her own sour air. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
